One of the things I love about singing this song by Rabbi David Zeller is that as we sing the words “I am alive,” the tune can feel mournful and aching in its celebration of aliveness. 

So I have to wonder, what is aliveness if not to include the great sorrows and aches of the heart and body? What is aliveness if not to include the pain of the world around us? What is aliveness if not to include death and destruction? 

As the song asks “And who is this aliveness that I am?” - I have to ask, aren’t we, too, blessed and holy by the painful magnitude of it all? “Is it not the blessed holy one?” Aren’t we created in the image of the whole? Do we trust ourselves to hold the whole, to be the whole, to accept this greatness? 

My heart goes out to all of you hurting now - whether in grief for the fires, the wars, the hostages, the ceasefire, the impending leadership of America, every country and its craziness, climate change, the future, your children, our children- and all the other reasons you want to delete this instagram app asap. All of this aliveness that feels like too much. 

My heart goes out to you. I pray we can always find a way to sing through this grief, to sing a pathway through the dark. 

From my aching heart, to yours. ❤️‍🩹

Hello, my name is Sonola Segun, a passionate public health graduate living with sickle cell disease. Despite facing numerous health challenges, financial struggles, and personal losses, I remain committed to making a positive impact on public health and environmental sustainability.

Why This Recycling Business Matters

Living with sickle cell disease has shown me how environmental factors—like pollution and poor waste management—can worsen chronic health conditions. A cleaner, healthier environment isn’t just a luxury; it’s essential. That’s why I’m launching a recycling business focused on:

Reducing waste

Educating communities about recycling and sustainability

Promoting better waste management practices

What I’ve Achieved So Far

I’ve already secured:

Permits & Licenses

Business Registration

Collection Containers

Sorting Equipment

Educational Flyers & Training Materials

However, I need your support to take the final steps and make this vision a reality.

What Your Support Will Fund

To launch effectively, I need help with:

1. Manual Sorting Costs: $390

2. Shredders: $3,000

3. Truck/Van for Transportation: $2,000

Total Goal: $5,390

The Impact of Your Contribution

Your donation will:

Create local jobs and boost the economy

Raise awareness about recycling and public health

Improve waste management practices

Reduce environmental damage caused by poor waste disposal

Join Me in Building a Greener Future

Every contribution, no matter how small, brings us closer to a cleaner, healthier world. Together, we can create an initiative that benefits public health, the environment, and future generations.

Thank you for your support and belief in this mission.

Sincerely,
Sonola Segun
Public Health Advocate & Sustainability Entrepreneur

🌱 Agroforestry: A Sustainable Farming Solution 🌳

Agroforestry combines trees with crops or livestock, creating a powerful way to boost farm productivity while protecting the environment. 🌿

🌳 Soil Health: Trees help reduce soil erosion, improve water retention, and maintain soil fertility. 
💧 Environmental Impact: Agroforestry reduces greenhouse gas emissions and nutrient loss, enhancing biodiversity. 
📈 Economic Benefits: In regions like Western Uttar Pradesh, farmers are seeing a benefit-cost ratio of up to 3:1 through systems that integrate trees like neem and eucalyptus.

Agroforestry is paving the way for more sustainable and profitable farming! 🌍

As Illinois moves toward a cleaner, more sustainable energy future, communities like St. Charles face a pivotal decision that could impact their environmental and financial landscape for decades. Currently, St. Charles, along with other municipalities, is bound by a long-term contract with Illinois Municipal Electric Agency (IMEA) that provides 80% of our power from 2 coal plants. The Prairie State coal plant in Illinois is one of the nation's largest coal polluters and is the #1 CO2 polluters in Illinois. This facility not only contributes millions of tons of CO2 annually but also produces dangerous coal ash that threatens water supplies and public health.

This educational event will explore the pressing decision facing St. Charles: extending its contract with IMEA  for 20 more years, potentially locking the city into coal-powered electricity until 2055. Attendees will learn about the environmental and health risks associated with Prairie State, the growing availability of cleaner, more affordable energy alternatives, and the role of technology in shaping a sustainable future.

Together, we’ll explore how St. Charles can lead the way in making forward-thinking decisions that not only protect the environment but also strengthen our local economy. By making informed, thoughtful choices today, we can create a future where clean energy powers our homes, supports our health, and ensures a thriving community for generations to come.

Key Questions to Address:
- Is there a business case for St. Charles to sign a new 20 year contract now, 10 years prior to the effective date?
- How can St. Charles avoid locking into a contract that may not be in the community's best interest long-term?
- What are the environmental and health impacts of continuing to rely on the Prairie State Coal Plant?
- What cleaner, cheaper energy options are available as technology advances?

Join us for this important discussion and learn how St. Charles can lead the way toward a cleaner, healthier energy future for all. This is a chance to ensure the city's energy decisions align with the rapidly evolving energy landscape.

Kandi Mossett White stood on the land where her ancestors were buried and watched as Energy Transfer bulldozed their graves to make way for the Dakota Access Pipeline. She saw it with her own eyes. The destruction wasn’t “alleged”—it happened. Yet now, a North Dakota jury has ruled in favor of Energy Transfer, awarding them millions in a defamation lawsuit against Greenpeace for saying exactly that.

This isn’t justice. This is corporate power using the legal system to rewrite history. Greenpeace stood with the Standing Rock Sioux to defend sacred land and water. Now they’re being punished for telling the truth.

This case isn’t just about Greenpeace—it’s about silencing anyone who dares to challenge the fossil fuel industry’s destruction of Indigenous lands. If this ruling stands, it could bankrupt one of the world’s leading environmental defenders and set a terrifying precedent for corporate attacks on activists.

Renowned human rights lawyer Steven Donziger—who helped hold Chevron accountable for poisoning Indigenous communities in the Amazon—is fighting back. His team is exposing the deep flaws in this case and mobilizing to defend Greenpeace from this blatant abuse of power.

“This is an abomination,” says Donziger. “It’s not just an attack on Greenpeace—it’s an attack on the entire movement for justice. We must fight back.”

We cannot let fossil fuel billionaires erase the truth. Greenpeace is under attack not because they did anything wrong, but because they did what was right.

Stand with Kandi. Stand with Greenpeace. Stand for the truth.

Donate if you can. Share this message. Make sure the world knows what really happened.

Will you fight back against this corporate assault on justice?

Louisiana LNG terminal plans massive expansion – and neighbors are fearful 

A new liquefied natural gas export terminal south of New Orleans, already among the world’s largest, is proposing an expansion that would double its size – and neighbors and environmental groups are worried about the project’s safety risks and pollution. 

“It’s a lot of risk. What if this thing explodes?” asked Wilkie DeClouet, a 60-year-old former chemical plant worker who lives in Plaquemines Parish, down the road from the Venture Global Plaquemines LNG terminal on the Mississippi River. “If it explodes, it’s going to be catastrophic.” 

He explained that his community is protected from the Mississippi River by dirt barriers called levees, which are crossed by gas pipelines supplying the LNG terminal. If one of the pipelines exploded, DeClouet worried, “it’ll blow the levee and flood this whole part of Louisiana.”
 
The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) is holding community meetings on the proposed expansion of the Plaquemines LNG terminal at 5 p.m. on Aug. 12 at the Belle Chasse Auditorium, at 8398 LA-23, in Belle Chasse, LA; and at 5 p.m. the next day at the Port Sulfur YMCA at 278 Civic Dr., in Port Sulphur, according to a FERC public notice.

Santa Fe Workshops collection of free online events, Creativity Continues, features an evening with world-renowned Brazilian photojournalist Victor Moriyama for a presentation on the environmental and sociological impacts of climate change in the Amazon rainforest.

During our evening together, Victor shares stories and insights from his long-term project documenting the violent occupation of the Amazon, shedding light on such issues as deforestation, land grabbing, and the vital role of Indigenous peoples in preserving the biome. His work tells the stories of the people, land, and animals directly impacted by these processes, and sheds light on the political, cultural, and environmental factors at play. Victor also discusses the challenges faced in journalism by those documenting climate change and reflects upon the importance of photographers listening to their inner voice in search of the issues that move us.

Sharon Schmeckel, a Wisconsin farmer, shared with us how she found solar energy to be a lifeline for her family farm. Balancing crop and cattle operations, solar has helped her extend the farm’s future while providing steady income and less physical strain. Here’s her story in her own words.👇💬🌾
“I have 40 acres of solar on my farm. We have alternating corn and soy as well as beef cattle. My husband and I were approached for the project 7 years ago and our biggest factor for signing up was economics. Farming isn’t that lucrative but solar panels are. I wanted to extend the viability of my farm - my husband passed away and my son works on the farm but also lost his leg in an accident and is limited in what he can do. This project has allowed us to continue the farm for another generation. Having solar is a lot less work, it saves time, and there’s no big machinery involved.
When the contract was brought to us we had our lawyer go over it. There were some minor things proposed that they agreed to so we were satisfied with the end result. The project is operational now and I’m glad I decided to sign on. I’ve always been pro-renewable energy. I support anything we can do to get off of oil and coal and replace that by making our own clean energy.”
-Sharon Schmeckel, Wisconsin Farmer.

Profs and Pints Napa presents: “Hunker in Bunkers, Escape to Space,” on the history and status of Americans’ responses to existential threats, with Emily Ray, associate professor of political science at Sonoma State University and co-author of Prepared: Doomsday Prepping in the United States.

[6 pm September 22nd at Napa Yard in Napa, California.]

Back in the middle of the 20th century, when we first became a nuclear world, the federal government encouraged American citizens to respond to threats of atomic annihilation by taking up doomsday preparation and bunker acquisition. 

Today most regard doomsday prepping as a fringe activity, albeit one gaining in popularity. But Professor Emily Ray of Sonoma State University argues that doomsday preparation remains foundational to U.S. politics and culture and is a key driver of the new space race. 

Gain insight into how much our lives, politics and culture are shaped by existential threats and survivalist fantasies by coming to Napa Yard, the new Napa Valley home of events offered by Profs and Pints, a social enterprise that democratizes access to higher learning.

Professor Ray will discuss the history of doomsday prepping in the US, from calls to grow war gardens to federal recommendations for surviving atomic bombs to today’s quest to find ways to inhabit other planets. She’ll discuss how figures like Jeff Bezos and Elon Musk have plans to colonize Mars partly to save humanity from worsening conditions on Earth, and how investment in the new space race is tied to climate change and political instability.

A scholar of environmental political theory and politics focused on the intersections of technology, outer-space policy, climate change, and social theory, Dr. Ray joined Prepared co-author Robert Kirsch of Arizona State University in developing the novel theory of “bunkerization” to explain our responses to collective existential threats. That theory and her upcoming talk deal with a profound question: How do we hope to address collective problems together when the solutions offered are focused on individual needs? 

Her talk might leave you rethinking how you prepare for the worst. (Tickets available only online. Advance tickets: $13.50 plus processing fees. Doors: $17, or $15 with a student ID. Doors open at 5:30 and the talk begins at 6:30.)

How do we tell our children that they may have less epiphanies in their lifetimes because we have blocked a view of the stars? 

Right now, over 80% of humanity has a polluted view of the night sky. (Falchi, 2016*) This 2016 citation is only more true 9 years later. The world doesn’t yet know about this silent, insidious, but very fixable aspect of climate change.

Across time, skygazing has inspired epiphanies. To look at a distance star is to meet the edges of human understanding. Just to touch this point in space with your mind is a superpower for problem-solving. 

We do not sit in darkness to find what we know, but rather to find what we do not know. When you feel this infinity, it is possible to see the narrowness in which you came upon your problem in the first place, and to once again remember that the infinity of all possibility remains. 

When we stop spending time in darkness, we stop exploring the full palette of human perception. 

✨Follow, like, and SHARE. Please help spread the word.✨

*https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.1600377

Esta innovadora iniciativa enfrenta de manera directa desafíos globales como el cambio climático, mediante una optimización meticulosa del uso del agua y el fortalecimiento de la biodiversidad. De este modo, asegura el futuro de nuestras vides, a través del manejo adecuado de nuestros viñedos antiguos, actuales y futuras plantaciones. Este esfuerzo pionero refuerza el liderazgo global de Viña Don Melchor, integrando de forma armoniosa la tradición con prácticas sostenibles para futuras generaciones. 

• Capítulo 5: Vinhedo Solar

Um passo audacioso rumo ao futuro da viticultura. Esta iniciativa inovadora enfrenta de forma direta desafios globais como a mudança climática, por meio da otimização meticulosa do uso da água e do fortalecimento da biodiversidade. Assim, garante o futuro de nossas vinhas, através do manejo cuidadoso de nossos vinhedos antigos, atuais e das futuras plantações. Este esforço pioneiro reforça a liderança global da Viña Don Melchor, integrando de maneira harmoniosa a tradição com práticas sustentáveis para as próximas gerações.

• Chapter 5: Sundial Vineyard

A bold step toward the future of winemaking. This innovative initiative directly addresses global challenges such as climate change through the meticulous optimization of water use and the strengthening of biodiversity. In doing so, it secures the future of our vines by carefully managing our old, current, and future vineyards. This pioneering effort reinforces Viña Don Melchor’s global leadership, harmoniously integrating tradition with sustainable practices for generations to come.

Texas is breaking new ground in clean energy production, with record highs in wind, solar, and battery power generation. In early March, the ERCOT power grid set new benchmarks, generating 28,470 megawatts from wind, 24,818 megawatts from solar, and discharging 4,833 megawatts from batteries. Battery storage has expanded significantly, now supplying nearly as much power as the state’s nuclear plants. While Texas has attempted to encourage the construction of new gas power plants, solar and battery installations have surged ahead, adding about 1 gigawatt per month over the past year.

On March 2, renewable sources accounted for a record 76% of ERCOT’s electricity demand during peak conditions. Later that evening, as solar production declined, batteries stepped in to supply over 10% of the grid’s total demand for the first time, offsetting a shortfall of more than 23,000 megawatts from thermal power plants, many of which were offline for maintenance or unplanned outages. Unlike traditional power plants, battery storage requires little maintenance and can provide backup support year-round.

Ideal weather conditions, with ample sunlight and strong winds, contributed to these renewable energy milestones. However, concerns remain about the grid’s stability during seasonal transitions when many fossil fuel plants are offline. Unexpected heat waves in spring, like the one in April 2006 that led to rolling blackouts, pose a growing risk as temperatures continue to rise.

The rapid expansion of solar and battery power has already helped Texas manage extreme summer heat and lower energy costs. Now, these technologies are demonstrating their ability to keep the grid stable even when conventional power sources are unavailable.

The anthropogenic warming of the earth often seems abstract, so let’s get more specific and nerdy together on this topic. 🌎🌱🤓🍃 The science is super interesting!

There is currently 427 ppm CO2 in the atmosphere, yet if current emission trends continue, it is projected that the world could reach 700 ppm of carbon dioxide (CO₂) in the atmosphere by the late 21st century—likely between 2075 and 2100. This projection depends on factors such as global energy policies, fossil fuel use, and efforts to reduce emissions.

The last time the earth had 700 ppm in the atmosphere, what was earth like? 

Comparable conditions in Earth’s past occurred during the Middle Miocene Epoch around 15 million years ago. 

Climate of the Middle Miocene Epoch 🔥 ~>

Average Global Temperature 🌡️

The average global temperature was about 65°F, which is approximately 5.4°F warmer than today’s average temperature of around 59°F (15°C). Climate models predict global temperatures would rise between 5-7°F or more above pre-industrial levels for us under this scenario. 

Sea Levels 🌊

Sea levels during the Miocene Epoch were estimated to be 50-80 feet higher than today. This increase was due to reduced polar ice, especially in Antarctica, where much of the ice had melted due to the warmer climate.

This level of warming would likely have profound impacts, including:

1. More frequent and severe heatwaves, droughts, floods, and storms.

2. Ecosystem Disruption: Further loss of biodiversity, particularly in sensitive ecosystems like coral reefs and polar regions.

3. Agricultural and Food Security Challenges: Changes in rainfall patterns, temperature extremes, and more intense droughts could affect crop yields and food supply.

The process of sea-level rise could take centuries to fully manifest, even after CO₂ levels stabilize. This would be a substantially different world for our grandchildren and great-grandchildren. 

Did you know about the Middle Miocene Epoch?

Let's talk about Promise #5 - Invest in Our Kids and Families

Our civic center was built in 1971—over 50 years ago—and it shows. Meanwhile, our youth sports fields are patchy, uneven, and poorly maintained, even as our programs continue to grow.

Leeds families are looking for safe, clean, and modern places to gather, play, and connect. But what they see instead are:

- Parks with no restrooms, and the few public bathrooms we do have are poorly maintained

- Facilities that haven’t been kept up—making it hard to take pride when we host visiting teams

Meanwhile, neighboring towns have nice, well-kept parks, community centers, and sports facilities; and they’re attracting families and small businesses because of it.

These things matter when you're building a community that’s proud, engaged, and growing. People want to live, work, and invest in places that show they care.

💰 Let’s Talk About the Money -
Leeds is carrying enormous debt. So what do we have to show for it?
Not a modern civic center.
Not quality recreational spaces.
Not consistently maintained parks or public areas that show we value our residents.

It’s fair to ask: Where did the money go?
Here’s the truth:
 ➡️ Too much has gone to consultants, lawyers, developer giveaways, and bad bond deals.
 ➡️ Not enough has gone into improving everyday life for the people who live here.

✅ How We Fix It
1. Get Serious About Parks and Recreation
- Develop a Parks Master Plan with real timelines and community input
- Prioritize fixing neglected parks and public spaces
- Build and maintain modern facilities 

2. Stop the Waste
- End or renegotiate corporate giveaways that don’t give back
- Cut excessive consultant and legal spending
- Say no to bond deals that don’t benefit residents directly

3. Invest in the Heart of the Community
- Fund facilities that support youth, families, and small business events
- Maintain and improve what we already own
- Build transparency: show residents where every dollar is going

Leeds has great people and great programs—but we’ve neglected the places those programs are supposed to happen.

It’s time to match our community’s energy with real investment in the public spaces we share.
🌳 Well-kept parks and facilities aren’t luxuries—they’re signs of a city that values its people.
🛠 Let’s stop wasting money and start building a better Leeds, together.

Wildfire smoke can affect air quality in areas thousands of miles away from active wildfires. Monitoring air quality indexes and advisories are crucial to minimizing exposure to harmful pollutants. Just as you check the weather forecast each day, check the air quality index for your area. 
The U.S. Air Quality Index (AQI) measures air pollution according to the Clean Air Act of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). The AQI has six color-coded categories to help you quickly determine the air quality in your community. 
• Green — Air pollution poses little to no risk, and the air quality is satisfactory.
• Yellow — Air quality is acceptable, but there may be a risk for people sensitive to air pollution.
• Orange — While most people are unlikely to be affected, people sensitive to air pollution may experience health effects. 
• Purple — The risk of health effects is increased for everyone. 
• Maroon — Everyone is likely to be affected by the hazardous conditions.

2025 Eat More Veggies, Save the Earth– Creative Short Video Contest

Purpose of the Contest

This is a small and heartwarming global event aimed at promoting the idea that eating more plant-based meals (not necessarily going fully vegan) can help slow down global warming. Every additional plant-based meal is a step toward protecting our Earth.

Contest Rules

Eligibility

Open to participants from all around the world, regardless of age or background.

Video Content

Create an original short video (within 3 minutes) based on the theme:

"Eating more plant-based meals helps reduce global warming."

Video content can be in any language.

Posting Guidelines

Post your video on your personal Instagram account.

Tag @方便素 in the post.

Include the hashtags: #方便素 and #多吃素救地球and#2025 Eat More Veggies, Save the Earth – Video Creativity Contest

In the comments section, write: "我愛地球，地球愛我" (I love the Earth, and the Earth loves me)

plus a five-character personal reflection in Chinese.

Video Creation

You may use AI tools to assist in your creation, but the content must be original and aligned with the contest theme.

Content Standards

Submissions must not include obscene, violent, hateful, or inappropriate content.

Entries that violate this rule will be disqualified.

Judging Criteria

The winner will be determined based on the highest number of comments on their Instagram post.

The top entry will receive a prize of NTD 30,000 (approx. USD 1,000).

Fairness and Anti-Cheating Policy

To maintain fairness, if there is evidence of comment manipulation, bot usage, or any other dishonest behavior,

the organizers reserve the right to adjust rankings or disqualify entries.

If you are not comfortable with this policy, please refrain from participating.

For any inquiries, please contact us in Chinese or English.

Contest Period

Start: August 22, 2025 at 10:00 AM (Taiwan Time)

End: September 21, 2025 at 5:00 PM (Taiwan Time)

Note: Videos posted before the official start time will not be eligible.

Winner Announcement

Winners will be announced on October 10, 2025.

During the contest period, the top 3 videos (by comment count) will be showcased weekly.

📐 Introducing Victoria Saracco — Our Newest 39er

If you've ever stood in the shadow of a building and felt the spirit of a city embedded in its walls, then you've already felt the kind of work Victoria believes in.

A born-and-raised Galvestonian, Victoria is an architectural designer whose passion for sustainable, human-scale design was shaped by childhood memories in her grandparents’ loft in the Old Daily News Building—a structure that weathered the 1900 storm, Hurricane Ike, and now, the next generation’s vision for Galveston’s future.

At just 25, she’s already thinking beyond blueprints. Victoria sees our island not as a relic or tourist stop, but as a place with untapped potential for live-work communities, revitalized commercial spaces, and design that reflects the soul of the people who live here—not just those who visit.

She's deeply rooted in Galveston's story—from MREs after the storm to over-the-head headphones at MOD—bringing both resilience and edge to every space she touches.

Welcome, Victoria. We're honored to have your voice, your vision, and your velocity as a part of the 39ers.

This is the new Galveston. And she is building it.

Hi my name is Josh Lucas and I'm proud to announce that I'm running to be the next Mayor of Fort Worth—From the perspective of someone who was born and raised in Fort Worth we are a world class city, but we don't have world class leadership.  I’m an uncle, a Democrat, and someone deeply committed to protecting our democracy and our community from the rising tide of right-wing extremism.Fort Worth is now the 11th largest city in the U.S. and the seat of Tarrant County. It’s time we start leading with compassion, equity, and a vision for progress.

As your mayor, I’ll fight to attract businesses that create real jobs with fair wages and benefits for our residents. But economic growth means little if we ignore the systemic issues harming our community. Since 2017, our county jail—just across the street from Tarrant County Community College—has reported 69 deaths while in custody, this is unacceptable. As your mayor I’ll advocate for a Mental Health Crisis Emergency Center as part of a robust jail diversion program, ensuring that vulnerable people are treated with care, not locked away indefinitely. 

We’ll also invest in our youth by reforming our Juvenile Justice system, centering programs around Fort Worth’s rich cultural arts. Every child deserves opportunities for growth, creativity, and success—regardless of their zip code or family income. And let’s not forget the basics: fixing potholes, improving public transit, and building sustainable infrastructure. These aren’t partisan issues—they’re about serving our community effectively. 

Democratic leadership means prioritizing people over profits, equity over exclusion, and progress over stagnation. When we lift up the most vulnerable among us, we create a stronger, more united Fort Worth.

Together, we can build a city that reflects our shared values—a city where everyone has the chance to thrive, and y'all really means all.

TOP STORY: New research revealing high levels of mercury in Texas bay raises alarms about dredging for oil tankers 

A scientist’s research showing high levels of mercury in seafood and sediment in a Texas bay is raising concerns that a proposed dredging project to benefit an expanding oil terminal would release more mercury into the bay. 

The proposed expansion of the Seahawk Oil Terminal in Point Comfort is part of an oil export boom along the Gulf Coast that has unfolded since Congress in 2015 reversed a ban on U.S. crude oil exports. The Port Lavaca project is one of 15 oil terminal projects across the country – including nine new export terminals planned, five expansions, and one under construction, according to public records compiled in the Oil & Gas Watch database. 

Jessica Dutton, a marine toxicologist and associate professor of biology at Texas State University, described her research findings about mercury in Lavaca Bay during an August 22 community meeting in Port Lavaca. 

Dutton's research found high levels of the neurotoxin not only in sediment at the bottom of Lavaca Bay, located about two hours southwest of Houston, but also in nearly half of some fish species caught in part of the estuary. 

Her study comes in the context of a massive proposed U.S. Army Corps of Engineers dredging project to widen, lengthen, and deepen a shipping channel that cuts through the bay to allow larger and more heavily loaded oil tankers to access the terminal. Local fishermen and environmental advocates are concerned that dredging will stir up mercury in the bay and allow it to accumulate in fish, shrimp, crabs, oysters, and other wildlife.  

Read the full story at news.oilandgaswatch.org.

A Ban on Large Modern Farms Was Stopped in Sonoma County, California

In November 2024, Sonoma County residents voted against a proposed ban on large modern farms. Created by Direct Action Everywhere (DxE), an anti-animal agriculture group known for trespassing on private agribusinesses and stealing livestock and poultry, Measure J threatened to permanently ban most modern farms and animal husbandry practices in Sonoma County.

Local farmers came together with early voting on the ban, showing an 85% “no” vote on Measure J, according to Dayna Ghirardelli, executive director of the Sonoma County Farm Bureau, in a KQED press release. Support also came from outside the local farming community, with campaign spending on Measure J reaching $2 million.

At AGPROfessionals, we also stepped up to advocate for our animal agriculture clients in response to the Measure J ban as well as the proposed bans in Denver, Colorado. Our communications team produced original articles and boosted them on our social media outlets. We also shared articles and notices produced by livestock association groups and other partners. Over the last number of months, our efforts reached tens of thousands of voters in each demographic area.

Measure J Would Have Impacted All of Animal Agriculture in the County

According to the Sonoma County Voter Information Guide, animals included in the Measure J ballot initiative were cattle, mature dairy cattle, veal calves, swine, horses, sheep, laying hens, broilers, and ducks, making it clear that no animal agribusiness in Sonoma County would have been exempt. If Measure J were to have passed, all large modern farms in Sonoma County would need to be “phased out,” and future developments of large modern farms would be “prohibited.” Current operations would have had three years to “phase-out” or meet Best Management Practices set by the county Agricultural Commissioner. Failure to cease or comply within the three years would have resulted in fines starting at $1,000 for the first day, $5,000 for the second day, and $10,000 every day in “violation” thereafter. In addition to fines, non-complying large modern farms would have also faced other enforcement available by law and at the discretion of the Agricultural Commissioner.

Family Farms Would Have Been Impacted

Opponents of the ban pointed out that the bill targeted multi-generational farming families in Sonoma County. According to an American Farm Bureau fact sheet about agriculture and food, in 2022, 95% of all United States farms are family owned.

If it passed, Measure J would have shut down large modern farms housing 89,000 cattle, 13,508 sheep, 2,935 hogs, 4,362 goats, and 225,496 laying hens, pullets, and broilers, according to the 2023 Sonoma County Crop Report.

Measure J Proponents Used Disinformation to Sway Voters

The verbiage on the ordinance claimed only “industrialized factory farms” would be prohibited and not “family farms.” This claim by the writers of Measure J was wholly disingenuous and misleading. Measure J proponents also claimed large modern farms were “major polluters and the breeding ground for diseases.” This statement was also false, as Sonoma County farmers are held to high animal welfare standards and environmental regulatory compliance. Family farms in Sonoma County use best practices to protect water quality and other environmental impacts. The coalition opposing Measure J ensured voters were aware that “Sonoma County’s farms are among the most progressive in the nation, setting high standards in sustainability and animal welfare. Our farmers are leaders in animal care, carbon sequestration, and environmental stewardship, practices that will be undermined by Measure J.”

The Group Behind Measure J – Was Also Behind the Slaughterhouse and Fur Bans in Denver

Founded in 2013 by Wayne Hsuing, a lawyer who subscribes to animal rights and environmental extremist ideology, Direct Action Everywhere (DxE) engages in extreme tactics to attack animal agriculture. An article by Protect The Harvest outlined some of the tactics used by DxE, including committing crimes such as theft of animals, which they call “open rescue,” and focusing on small organic farms that do not have the resources to fight back against DxE activities. DxE’s “open rescue” policy includes breaking into and stealing animals from farms and ranches, which risks the health and welfare of the livestock they steal and the livestock on the premises. According to the same article, the organization also targets small organic farms to further its “all animal agriculture is abuse” narrative.

Pro-Animal Future (PAF) is an anti-animal agriculture group connected with DxE. Based out of Denver, Colorado, PAF is a sub-group of the Phauna Foundation, a 501 c (3). Its website states that the organization is “known in the animal rights community for its studies on public attitudes regarding the use of animals for food.” According to an article by Protect The Harvest, PAF used its influence to further its vegan agenda in the Denver area to get similar initiatives to Measure J on the ballot. The ballot initiatives targeted animal processing facilities and the sale of fur products in Denver city and county.

The Ultimate in Sustainability and Animal Welfare

Today’s farmers and ranchers operate on very thin margins. Animals that are stressed or unhealthy do not produce well. Therefore, animal welfare and efficiency are top priorities for producers. With this in mind, university researchers and livestock veterinarians have developed modern farming practices in conjunction with producers who have generations of practical knowledge and experience. The practices employed by large modern farms help ensure the animals' health and well-being, reduce inputs, and make the most of land, water, and other resources.

Not only are large modern farms, primarily owned by families, feeding the country, but more and more are doing so utilizing sustainable energy. Some examples of the use of renewable energy on farms and ranches include geothermal, solar panels, windmills, hydro systems, and methane digesters. In 2022, 153,101 farms and ranches used renewable energy. This is a 167% increase from 2012 when only 57,299 farms used on-farm renewable energy practices.

Large Modern Farms Support Local Economies

The latest statistics from the American Farm Bureau Federation show that there are 1.9 million farms and ranches in the US, comprising less than 2% of the population. Family-run operations account for more than 95% of total agricultural production value. One large modern farm, on average, feeds 169 people in the U.S. and abroad annually.

In Sonoma County, large modern farms support the local, state, and national economy by adding tens of millions of dollars in revenue each year. According to the 2023 Sonoma County Crop Report, the livestock industry in Sonoma County contributed $39,489,100 to the local economy in 2023.

Looking Toward the Future

In her article about the recent ballot initiative in Sonoma County, Hannah Weeman Thompson from Animal Agriculture Alliance emphasized the importance of staying abreast of threats to animal agriculture and turning out to vote “no” when these initiatives appear on the ballot. We must stand up against groups like DxE and PAF and stop bans on large modern farms and slaughterhouses from gaining traction. “Losses today are unlikely to deter activists,” Weeman said, “as they believe ‘even if none of them pass, it’s going to set a precedent, we’ve already won by getting these on the ballot.’”

Thankfully, Measure J did not pass, and neither did the bans in Denver. However, despite this defeat at the polls, the threat to large modern farms is far from over. The groups vying to end animal agriculture are motivated by their ideology and they have a long game.

Links to references can be found in this article on our website

From the Wisconsin State Journal:  
Julia Henley-- District 41
 

Julia Henley, a Democrat from Blue River, wants to protect the beauty of the Driftless Area while encouraging rural development in sustainable ways. Her background in construction and design, as well as flood relief, will bring knowledge and ideas to state debates over regulation. Henley faults state leaders for “starving” schools in the district, which includes Wisconsin Dells, Reedsburg and Richland Center. She also faults Republicans who control the Legislature for failing to take federal Medicaid dollars, which she says could help keep more rural hospitals open. Henley favors abortion rights, unlike her opponent, Rep. Tony Kurtz, R-Wonewoc, who didn’t meet with our editorial board. She also favors legalizing small amounts of marijuana, just as surrounding states have, while being sure to educate young people about the risks. Henley pledged to be a “bridge-builder” in the Legislature if elected. She said she learned how to pull people together while leading a nonprofit. She would be a more independent advocate for her constituents.

Thanks! J:)

The truth about industrial farming is hard to swallow. For decades, we’ve been led to believe that mass-produced food is the key to feeding the world’s growing population. But the reality is stark: industrial farming is destroying us.

It’s destroying our soil, stripping it of its nutrients and leaving it barren and lifeless. It’s destroying our health, pumping our bodies full of toxic chemicals and processed foods that are linked to chronic diseases. And it’s destroying our planet, contributing to climate change, deforestation, and the loss of biodiversity.

But there’s hope. There’s a movement growing, a movement that’s rooted in the connection between healthy soil, healthy humans, and healthy animals.

On February 15th at 5pm, we invite you to join us for an exclusive viewing of Common Ground, a powerful documentary that exposes the truth about industrial farming and offers a solution.
 
So join us on February 15th at 5pm for this exclusive viewing of Common Ground. Let’s come together to create a better future for our planet, our health, and our communities.
